Now that we have completed the logic to create a folder and upload files to DMS, we will create the logic to start the SAP Build Process Automation process from this SAP Build Apps application. It will start with the uploaded file (folder) information attached, so you will be able to reference this file on SAP Build Process Automation.

In SAP Build Apps, you can use the process created in Part 1 with SAP Build Library from Integrations.

If you can't see the deployed process in the list here, you've probably forgotten to Publish.

Create the logic for the Execute Workflow button. The logic is simple.

The important thing is the "Trigger Process". Select the process you made available from Integrations.

Select Object with Properties as Input Parameters and use Formula to set the values.

As explained in Part 1, the folder ID information must be passed in the format "spa-res:cmis:folderid:<folder ID on DMS>". The folder ID should have been assigned to Application Variables from the API return value when creating the folder. Concatenate the string "spa-res:cmis:folderid:" to that value.

The logic is now complete. Let's test it.

 

Test

Run the Build Apps application you created.

Clicking the File Upload button will open a file selection dialog, so this time we will upload a PDF document. I created a simple PDF document in Word for testing purposes.

The display depends on the settings in the dialog set after the file upload API, but it looks like the upload was successful. In debugging mode, it is better to check whether the folder is created and uploaded properly using ReuseUI or DMS API.
Next, click the Execute Workflow button to start the SAP Build Process Automation process.
This process (created in Part 1) is a structure that sends a form with a file attached to itself after being triggered by the API. Open My Inbox from SAP Build Lobby.

You should have received the form yourself. There seems to be an attached file as well. Let's check the contents.

You can confirm that it is a PDF file that you uploaded from SAP Build Apps.

In this way, you can upload files with SAP Build Apps and link them to the SAP Build Process Automation workflow. SAP Build Apps allows you to perform more application-like processing than SAP Build Process Automation. It is also possible to do things that are impossible with SAP Build Process Automation forms, such as narrowing down searches such as major classification -> medium classification -> minor classification -> product, and interactive collaboration with external systems. The recommended usage is to use SAP Build Apps on the application screen that triggers the workflow.

nother important point is that you can link files uploaded to DMS from other apps by passing the format "spa-res:cmis:folderid:" + folder ID when triggering a process. (Although it needs to be the same DMS repository used by SAP Build Process Automation)

From what we hear from customers about how they use it, it seems like a common pattern is to attach files to workflows. I think you can do a lot more with it, so please give it a try.

Regards,

entry.title: SAP BNAC and SAP BPA: Integration and Process Design
entry.content:
Following this blog article, you will configure a SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration (BNAC) instance and integrate SAP Build Process Automation (BPA) via the provided BPA interfaces. In order to follow the relevant steps outlined below, please make sure to meet the following prerequisites.

Prerequisites

BTP Subaccount Entitlements

  • SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration (BNAC)
  • SAP Build Process Automation (BPA)
  • (Optional) SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

Instances & Subscriptions

  • SAP Asset Intelligent Network, standard edition (until the time of this writing)
  • Destination Service, lite
  • SAP Build Process Automation, standard edition
  • (Optional) SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

Cloud Foundry Runtime

  • At least on CF space for service deployment (e.g. dev)

Setup

Assuming the scenario, the services and instances mentioned above are deployed on a new BTP subaccount. In this case, additional configurations must be made to ensure proper functioning of the applications.

SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration (BNAC)

Beginning with the SAP BNAC application. After initial creation of subscription and the according instance, BNAC requires a so called "object prefix", which is used for populating the underlying database schema. In order to set this prefix, go to Application Settings > General Application Settings and set the Object Prefix within the dedicated input field.

Optional: Due to its purpose as network application, BNAC allows the sharing of business objects within a certain group of business partners. To invite a business partner, please go to the application Business Partners on the BNAC dashboard and search for the relevant business partners using the search options. Nevertheless, this step is only necessary in cases you want to share and receive business objects from other partners.

After taking these steps, the BNAC instance could be used to create and configure business objects.

SAP Build Process Automation (BPA)

The tool suite and integrations offered by SAP BPA is not only restricted to execute processes in an automated manner. Beside the possibility to connect mail servers for sending pre-defined e-mails, approval forms could be send via "My Inbox" in SAP Build Work Zone and so called agents allow offloading dedicated tasks from BPA.

For using the standard process steps offered by BPA, not all of these integrations are necessary. Due to the intention of this article to give you an overview of the integration possibilities, the required setup steps are described in the next sections. Feel free to choose the capabilities you want to use. Otherwise you could skip the next parts.

Mail Server

SAP BPA offers the possibility to send e-mails during the execution of a process. These mails differ into:

  • free form e-mails, received in the mailbox of your choice
  • approval forms, received within the inbox of SAP BPA (which requires SAP Work Zone)

To send mails in general, a mail server is required. Setting up this mail server and the corresponding mail address, which will be used by SAP BPA, the administrator must create a Destination called sap_process_automation_mail in the subaccount BPA lives in. This could be done within the BTP Cockpit in section Connectivity > Destinations. An example configuration to connect a GMAIL account with SAP BPA follows below: 

 

# Exported BTP Destination
mail.smtp.port=587
mail.bpm.send.disabled=false
mail.user=<YOUR-MAIL-ADDRESS>
mail.password=<YOUR-PASSWORD> or <CLIENT-CREDENTIAL>
mail.smtp.auth=true
mail.smtp.from=<YOUR-MAIL-ADDRESS>
mail.smtp.starttls.enable=true
mail.smtp.host=smtp.gmail.com
Name=sap_process_automation_mail
mail.smtp.ssl.enable=false
Type=MAIL
mail.smtp.ssl.checkserveridentity=false
mail.transport.protocol=smtp
Authentication=BasicAuthentication
mail.smtp.starttls.required=true
mail.description=Choose your own
ProxyType=Internet

 

After creating this Destination, SAP BPA should automatically source the variable and establish the connection to the defined SMTP server. The resulting configuration shown in Control Tower > Mail Server should look like this:

Overview of mail servers found by search for the configured Destination variable

Agent

Before the process modeller could use the Automation library, an agent needs to be configured within the used environment. In order to create such an agent, the subaccount administrator have to make sure, that the subaccount has the entitlement of automation-unattended with a minimum amount of 1 as part of process-automation entitlement. This allows the agent to execute dedicated tasks in the unattended mode. For further information about the topic of agents, please feel free to visit SAP Help Agent Documentation.

To connect an agent with SAP BPA, the agent software needs to be installed on a system connected to SAP BPA. During the creation process of this blog post, a local workstation (MacBook Pro) was utilised. To use this kind of automation within more productive settings, keep in mind that you could install the agent software on servers as well.

First of all, a technical user is required, which will later be assigned to the agent and serves as representation in front of the system. Therefore, visit the Repository-Based Shipment Channel (RBSC) and create a user. After choosing a name satisfying the naming conventions, click on the user within the user list and search for the item Strong Cryptography Authentication in the appeared listing. Click on the option Generate JWT and store the generated secret in a safe place for later usage.

Next, go to the Control Tower section within SAP BPA and go to the section Agent Configuration containing the Agents application. After a fresh install, the list of available agents should be empty. To change this, click on Go to Download Page in the top right corner. This leads to a page, which allows you to download the software packages required for installing the agent on a system of choice. Beside downloading the software, this page is an indirect installation instruction guiding you through the process:

  1. Install the Agent
    After downloading and installing the agent, you should see an icon as part of your title bar on your system. This ensures, that the installation process worked.
  2. Allow Extension
    Please close now all your browser instances and restart your browser. This will initiate the setup of the browser extension used by SAP BPA to debug processes with agents involved (in case you want the extension in private browsing windows as well, please configure this within the extension settings in your browser).
  3. Register
    Return to the page inside your browser and click on the *Register Agent* button. This will initiate the last step of the registration process within the agent on your local machine.

Optional: To manually register an agent with your SAP BPA instance, the Agent Configuration > Agents overview in the Control Tower offers the Register Agent button (beside the Go to Download Page button). Use the presented link and switch to your local agent. After clicking on the agent icon, a small window with all configuration options should appear. Here, click on the cloud icon in the bottom left corner and now on Add Tenant. Please paste the link from SAP BPA here.

Tenant configuration using the provided URL for manual registering

 

Configured agent connected to SAP BPA

After following the previous steps, the agent should be ready for usage in an environment of your choice. To finalise the agent setup, the agent must be assigned to an SAP BPA environment. Therefore, switch to Control Tower > Tenant Configuration > Environments. Select the preferred environment from the previewed listing (e.g. the default Public environment). In the environment overview page, select the tab Agent Management. Within this tab, use the button Add Agent in the top right corner and select your registered agent. Finally, you should have one agent listed within your environment.

Overview of configured agents linked to SAP BPA

Good to know: In order to utilise your configured agents, please make sure, that the version of the registered agent is equal or greater than the specified agent version in your processes.

Destinations

In order to call web service APIs, Destinations created on a subaccount level must be created upfront. To source their endpoint specific configuration (e.g. authentication, header information, ...) within a processes, SAP BPA requires an additional step to map internal Destination Variables onto BTP Subaccount Destinations. Following the principle of the previous integrations, the mapping process for Destinations within SAP BPA is done via the Control Tower. Arrived in this part of the application, select the Destinations tile as part of the section Backend Configuration.

Please make sure, you have created and configured the Destinations (BTP Subaccount level) you want to use for the mapping process. Otherwise, BPA may not find the entries and cannot add them to the instance. Assuming, the Destinations are correctly created and saved, click on the Add button beside Open in BTP Cockpit and select the Destinations pointing to the systems you want to contact. Furthermore, associate the desired environment (or per default associate them with all environments) as part of the next step and then click Add Destination. Now you should see the created *Destination* as part of the listing.

Mapped Destinations from BTP Subaccount

BNAC - BPA Integration

After the individual configuration of SAP BNAC and SAP BPA, this section guides you through the process of connecting both worlds with each other. Therefore, the first step is the creation of another Destination in the BTP subaccount.

Prerequisite: Make sure you have created a service key for SAP Build Process Automation instance within the BTP Cockpit. This information is used now for connecting both systems. The required details from the service key are indicated by the placeholder values used in the Destination template below.

 

# Exported BTP Destination
content-endpoint=https\://<SAP-BPA-URL>/workflow/rest
Type=HTTP
Description=Choose your own
User=<CLIENT-ID>
Password=<CLIENT-SECRET>
Authentication=BasicAuthentication
ProxyType=Internet
URL=https\://<SAP-BPA-UAA-URL>.hana.ondemand.com
Name=ac_workflow

 

The dedicated keys of the service key are outlined in the provided reference below.

Reference:

  • <SAP-BPA-URL>: Value could be found in "endpoints"."api" in the service key. Please do not forget to add the suffix '/workflow/rest'
  • <SAP-BPA-UAA-URL>: Value could be found in "uaa"."url" in the service key
  • <CLIENT-ID>: Value could be found in "uaa"."clientid" in the service key
  • <CLIENT-SECRET>: Value could be found in "uaa"."clientsecret" in the service key

Similar to the integration of a mail server within SAP BPA, SAP BNAC identifies this Destination automatically as a SAP BPA endpoint for starting mapped workflows after certain events are triggered. To map a workflow to an event type, use the Manage Workflow application accessible via the BNAC dashboard within the Administration section. The final result should be similar to the example provided afterwards, demonstrating a process mapped for the Create Equipment event type. This will trigger the mapped process each time an equipment is created within BNAC.

Example mapping of SAP BPA process to a "Create Equipment" event from SAP BNAC

Creating a Process

Based on the configurations made in the previous chapter, SAP BPA is capable of creating processes acting on the behalf of SAP BNAC and further process information without the attendance of a human user. As part of the underlying scenario, a process was created, which could be used to automate the sharing of business objects, equipments in this case, with already connected business partners.
A complete overview of the process, is shown in the Example process for sharing and publishing an equipmentattached picture. Basically, the process listens via an API Trigger on incoming requests from SAP BNAC after an equipment is created. Afterwards, the process queries SAP BNAC for further information about the equipment created using an Action. This information will be used to create an approval form, which requests confirmation by a specified person. After the person approves the received form request, the process uses the SAP BNAC API to publish the specified equipment and share it with a connected business partner. Finally, a free form e-mail is send out, that the sharing process succeeded. In case of an rejection, the specified persons are informed about the send out approval was rejected.

Process Trigger

By using SAP BNAC as the trigger instance for the modelled processes, the first event must be of type API Trigger. As defined defined in the Destination ac_workflow, created in the previous section, SAP BNAC calls this URL after the specified event type is fired. Beside solely calling the URL, SAP BNAC provides further information to SAP BPA. This includes the process which should be triggered combined with a context. The context describes some HTTP payload carried by the request, which could be used as process input. In case you want to dig deeper into this mechanism, please take a look at  this SAP Blog article.

SAP BPA offers, apart from the API Trigger, additional trigger types, which could be used. Nevertheless, for linking SAP BNAC with SAP BPA, you have to use the above mentioned type. Otherwise, the process will not start.

Process Input

After defining the trigger for the new process, process inputs must be defined. In this case, it is data coming from the calling application (BNAC) and which will be used in the subsequent steps. As already mentioned in the previous section, the incoming request carries some context containing certain information. By taking a closer look at the incoming request, the following context could be identified.

 

{
  ...
  "context": {
    "objectid": "<EQUIPMENT-ID>",
    "type": "<TYPE>",
    ...
  }
}

 

Input variables derived from the context send from SAP BNAC

Beside an objectid representing the business object, a type is specified. For this scenario, the process only requires the objectid to request additional information about the business object at the beginning of the process. To make this information accessible withinthe process flow, the configuration of process variables is necessary. Therefore, open the Process Details view (use the << button on the right hand side of the screen) and switch to the tab Variables. Now, click on the Configure button within the first section Process Inputs and provide the desired information. An example configuration for the provided context from BNAC is shown afterwards.

With this input variables in place, the process modeller could access this information throughout the whole process as an input for Actions, Forms, Approvals and Controls.

Process Modelling

To achieve the desired outcome, the process flow and the required process steps may differ from case to case. Nevertheless, this section shortly introduces some common steps which demand further configuration to work (e.g. Action, Automation).

Action Steps

By creating an Action, a library of HTTP requests will be created, which could be included into a process as a dependency. Like other artefacts in SAP BPA (e.g. SDK, Process), they could be published, shared and used by other SAP BPA users. As part of this section, the process of creating an Action calling the Equipment API of SAP BNAC is briefly explained. These steps could be adapted and adjusted for the requirements of the API to call.

At the beginning stands the creation of an Action via the SAP Build dashboard. To create a library, please go to the Connectors section in the side panel of BPA and select Actions. After an initial setup, the list of available actions should be empty. Now, click on Create in the top right corner. For the purposes of this use case, the built actions are configured from scratch. Therefore, choose the option Build from Scratch in the Choose an API Source popup. Provide a meaningful name and a description for the Action you want to create and click on Create. As a naming convention, the domain of the Action could be used (e.g. EquipmentActions similar to this case).

If everything worked out, a new browser tab will be opened and a tile to create the first HTTP request should appear. Based on the underlying scenario, let's create a request to retrieve further information about the created equipment using its identifier (via the objectid input variable):

  • Operation: GET
  • Name: Get Equipment Header
  • Description: Get the header information for a specified equipment
  • Endpoint: /equipment({id})/header

Now click on the Add button. You should be presented with a GET request with no parameters as input and no output. To specify an input, like indicated via {id} in the endpoint, click above the Parameter table on Add, choose New Field and provide the following configurations:

  • Key: id
  • Mandatory: YES
  • Parameter: path
  • Type: string
  • Label: Equipment ID
  • Static: NO
  • Description: Identifier for the equipment
  • Max Length: 32

After clicking on Add, the new parameter should be added to the parameter list.

HTTP GET Request for retrieving equipment information by ID

 

 

Next, the operation requires an output. Otherwise, the received content could not be used within the process. Therefore, switch to the tab Output and click on Edit List below the dropdown Choose Output. Within the appeared tile click on Add New Output and provide the name 200. Click on Save afterwards. Now, you should be able to create an output format via the Add button on the table with the Body header. This definition will be used as Data Type within the process the action is used.

For most of the cases, the definition of an output schema via the option Fields from Sample JSON is sufficient.  It allows to copy and paste the API response in JSON format from the actual endpoint and use it as blueprint for the resulting types. Apart from the minimisation of overhead resulting from the manual creation of the respective fields, this options also reduces potential mapping errors configuring large response bodies.

Using this approach, the creation of other requests as part of such a library works in a similar way. E.g. the creation of POST or PUT requests requires, in contrast to the created GET request, the additional configuration of a request body (in case you want to send data).

Good to know: To use the Action as part of the modelled process, the library must be released and published. Therefore, click on the Release button in the top right corner. Afterwards select the released version from the dropdown on the top left of the screen. By changing to the version, which is already released, the release button should change to Publish. After clicking on Publish the Action should be available for consumption in the processes within SAP BPA.

Action libraries linked to the implemented process

Automation Steps

Due to the fact, an Action only allows the usage of a static endpoint (defined in the Destination Variables in SAP BPA) and lacks the capability to pre- and post-process the transmitted data, an Automation could be used instead.

Prerequisites: To use this type of process step, an agent must be installed and configured in the SAP BPA instance, and at least the Core SDK must be installed in the process.

Similar to a sub-process step inside a parent process, the Automation allows the business modeller to create standalone reusable processes, which are executed by an agent. One example could be the calling of an API, which is briefly explained as part of this subsection.

For this particular case, the Automation should process the following steps:

  1. Custom script: Prepare data as a JSON body object and pass it down the process chain
  2. Call Web Service: Utilise the JSON object, call a web service API and pass the HTTP response down the process chain
  3. Custom script: Grab the response body from the previous step and return it as output from the Automation
  4. Wait: Wait a certain amount of time to continue

To implement the desired behaviour, the process directly starts with a Custom script control (1). This type of controls offer the possibility to craft functions, written in the programming language JavaScript, to process control inputs and the outputs accordingly. In order to pass values in and out of the controls, they must be defined as part of the Input Parameters and the Output Parameters of the control. This could be done by using the details view of the control after drag-and-drop'ing it into the modelling screen.

An example of creating a custom JavaScript object, which will be passed to the subsequent Call Web Service Activity is shown below.

 

var body = { ... };

return {                    // returns the object as output of the step
    url: 'URL',             // endpoint
    method: 'POST',         // specify HTTP method
    responseType: 'json',   // indicate response type
    resolveBodyOnly: true,  // resolve only the body, not the header values
    headers: {
        'key': 'value'      // header values
    },
    json: body              // to send body as JSON object and not as escaped JSON string
};

 

Good to know: During the process of creating this Automation, the Call Web Service with Destination Activity lacked the behaviour of adding header values to the corresponding requests. Therefore, the headers are added manually in the basic Call Web Service Activity.

The utilised syntax for the created JavaScript object is special to the Call Web Service Activity (2) and can be used directly by the activity without any further processing steps. Only make sure to reference the output of the Custom script control within the Activity. Otherwise, the web service will be called without the prepared parameters. Nevertheless, you could use this object by its name within all steps/Tools in the Automation.

The subsequent step to post-process the response body of the API call (3) works in the exact similar way like the Custom Script implemented at the beginning. So the utilised JavaScript code to map the response into another format will be omitted here. The final step to wait a certain amount of time (4) in order to proceed with the overall process is self-explanatory.

Process Deployment

Finally, the process must be released and deployed. Otherwise, SAP BNAC won't be able detect the process within the Manage Workflows application and no event type could be linked to the process. Therefore, make sure to save all artefacts referenced by the overall process and click on the Release button. During releasing the process, SAP BPA asks the modelling expert to select the Destination Variables, which should be used as endpoints for the Action steps. Therefore, the already configured Destinations should be selected. Afterwards, the released process version is available for deployment in the environments configured within SAP BPA.

To deploy the newest process version, similar to the publishing steps for an Action, select the released version from the dropdown in the middle of the page (tagged with the Release tag) and click on the Deploy button. After a short processing time, you should find your process version deployed in your default environment in SAP BPA.

Deployed process version after releasing

Link Event Type to Process

After the process is published and deployed, the SAP BNAC user is capable of configuring the automation of workflows vie the Manage Workflows application as part of the dashboard. To create a new mapping of an event type to a process, click on the New button in the top right corner of the screen and select the desired event type. After the selection of the event, a deployed process could be selected. Therefore, click on the respective input field and choose the process you want to map. 

Select event typeSelect SAP BPA process for mapping

entry.title: Convert DMS Folder ID to Name in Automation
entry.content:
Many people are having trouble working with DMS (SAP BTP Document Management System) in automations because when a user uploads a document via a form, SAP Build Process Automation only passes the object ID of the folder.

Problem is, the Get DMS Folder Content activity in automations needs a path.

You can solve this problem with just a few extra activities – I've done it in 3 steps, but you can probably do it in fewer. Note that all this assumes that the folder is in the root, which is where SAP Build Process Automation puts uploaded files.

To start, you will need a destination to your DMS repo. I just use the same one that I use to enable DMS in SAP Build Process Automation forms, the one called sap_process_automation_document_store.

The URL for mine is in this form:

https://api-sdm-di.cfapps.us10.hana.ondemand.com/browser/<repoID>

You will need to create an environmental variable of type destination, used to select the DMS destination. I called mine MyDMS.

And you need is an input variable of type string to your automation.

This variable will take from your process the string representation of the DMS folder that contains your document. The folder will be in the root of the repo and will be in this format:

spa-res:cmis:folderid:PH_jHhsadhewQ5ijlsakvwRcCOx5SbQYq8zorhf0K-I

 

Custom Script

The custom script is used to provide the definition of the API call we will make in the next activity. 

The script will have these inputs and outputs.

You will have to bind the input to the automation input above of the same name.

Here is the script:

 

 

let options = {
    method: 'GET',
    resolveBodyOnly: true,
    responseType: 'json'
};

if (folderId){
    options.url = "/root?objectId=" + folderId.split('folderid:')[1] + "&cmisselector=properties";
}

return options;

 

 

It is based on the Get Properties API for DMS.

 

Call Web Service with Destination

Add the Call Web Service with Destination activity, and set it up like you would for most such calls.

  • Set the input destination to your environmental variable.
  • Set the options input to output of your custom script. 
  • Keep the default output object, of obj.

 

Create Variable (String)

Now extract the name of the folder. Add a Create Variable activity (actually done by dragging a String data type into the flow).

All you need to do is create an input and output.

The input is the following formula, based on the output of the Call Web Service activity.

Step3.obj["cmis:name"].value

And the output can be any name you want.

Now you have the folder name.

😺

 

 

 

 

 

 

.

entry.title: Supercharge efficiency with Enterprise Automation: SAP Build and SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
The ability to intelligently automate business processes is top of mind for many stakeholders involved in process management across organizations. According to a recent IDC study, annual spending on Intelligent Process Automation (IPA) technologies is projected to reach $102 billion by 2028. Of this, $19.9 billion will be allocated to AI-powered automation tools, with an impressive annual growth rate of 43.4%.

While there will be certainly no shortage in resources to spend for automation software, the real challenge remains in the details: How can organizations implement a holistic approach for process excellence, which allows them to manage processes, identify bottlenecks, deliver scalable automations and integrate their process extension within a holistic process landscape. In my previous blogpost I have already outlined in detail how SAP’s framework on Enterprise Automation delivers an solution to the problem statement mentioned above.

And this blogpost series will offer you a more detailed guidance on the interconnectivity of each service included in Enterprise Automation which are:

  • SAP Build Process Automation
  • SAP Integration Suite
  • and of course SAP Signavio.

Let’s explore the value you can unlock for your processes by combining SAP Build Process Automation with SAP Integration Suite in this one.

 

What is Enterprise Automation?

As outlined in my previous blogpost Enterprise Automation with SAP: Enterprise automation with SAP software provides an integrated set of tools for process mining, automation, integration, monitoring, and optimization of your processes and workflows. It helps you quickly and intuitively gain insights into your business processes and identify the right automation tool for the right outcome. While each solution can be leveraged independently, Enterprise Automation offer native integration between SAP Build, SAP integration Suite and SAP Signavio – supplemented with best practices, guidance and pre-build content which can be implemented directly.

 

 

  • SAP Signavio provides visibility into business processes and potential bottlenecks, root-cause analysis, and suggestions for improvement based on 200,000 industry benchmarks and best practices. This detailed understanding of inefficiencies across your organization enables you to optimize and automate processes.
  • SAP Integration Suite is an integration platform as a service with thousands of prebuilt integrations, APIs, events and connectors to SAP and third-party software applications. The suite reduces complexity across on-premise and multi-cloud environments, helping you optimize comprehensive business processes.
  • SAP Build to create automations and applications usingthe low-code and no-code design tools. Including pre-built automations and connectors for SAP software and third-party systems, the solutions let your business experts rapidly address process inefficiencies

 

Here you can also see a visualization of the main components and its respective capabilities:

 

Leverage the combined power of SAP Build Process Automation and SAP Integration Suite

 

1st scenario: Integrate to innovate

The first scenario we are going to cover in this blogpost is about building required process extensions and integrating the to your systems of records. SAP Build Process Automation allows you to create entirely new business processes and automation from scratch. However, during development, you may need advanced integration capabilities and various types of connectors to link your processes with different systems, whether cloud-based or on-premise, and whether using SAP or third-party platforms.

Integration Suite, you can natively trigger an iFlow created in SAP Integration Suite at any point within your process definition. This is particularly useful when handling tasks like data transformation, entity mapping, field validation, or accessing over 250 connectors such as iDoc, SOAP, SMTP, Kafka, and more. Moreover, customers that start their process excellence journey with Integration Suite can complement their integration styles with additional automation capabilities like approval workflows, business rules or RPA bots that have been provisioned with SAP Build Process Automation.

Let’s jump into an example. In the screenshot below you can see a process definition within Build Process Automation to automate the creation of Purchase Requisitions within your organization:

 

 

Once all the parameters and required approvals are fulfilled, we want to create a PR document within our system of record. In the first Action of the screenshot below you can see that we are natively calling an OData service to directly push this new PR within our SAP S/4HANA on-premise system. But in the second Action, we trigger an additional Integration Flow (iFlow) that has been established within the SAP Integration Suite:

 

 

This is done rather quickly thanks to the ability to natively trigger an iFlow within the Action design-time of SAP Build Process Automation, which is also documented in our Help-Portal:

 

 

This allows you to include in iFlow within your process definition whenever it is necessary. Because in the iFlow design-time of the Integration Suite you can establish any scenario that is relevant, make use of data validation and entity mapping, and use more than 250 different connectors to integrate to any backend application. The connectivity options and adapters are also documented within the Help-Portal. In this screenshot you can see how the iFlow replicates the PR document in our MM-module of SAP ECC by using a SOAP adapter:

 And here you can see an example how entity mapping looks like: We are mapping the corresponding data fields of the OData service to the SOAP service to make sure that no data gets lost or misinterpreted.

 

 

2nd scenario: Event-driven architectures

Another common use case for combining SAP Build and SAP Integration Suite is in the area of event-driven architectures. Thanks to the Event Mesh service available in SAP Integration Suite, you can listen to and subscribe to critical business events, whether they are happening within your SAP or non-SAP environment. This also includes the new Advanced Event Mesh service, which offers more capabilities, broader deployment options, and the ability to handle larger payloads compared to the regular Event Mesh service.

SAP Build provides native connectivity to both Event Mesh and Advanced Event Mesh, enabling you to proactively trigger process instances without human intervention, based on business events as they occur.

For instance, you can use Advanced Event Mesh to listen for and subscribe to the creation of business partners within your S/4HANA system, as well as in third-party systems. Whenever a new business partner is created, SAP Build triggers a process instance to validate the data, sending approval notifications to business users to assign addresses and payment information for the new business partner, ensuring high data quality.

By integrating event-driven architectures with SAP Build, you can significantly increase the processing speed of your business workflows, reduce human intervention, and enhance operational excellence for your IT department.

Here you can see a screenshot of the Advanced Event Mesh cockpit within SAP Integration Suite:

 

You can listen and subscribe to multiple events by creating queues in your SAP or non-SAP system within multiple regions and hyperscalers. For example it can be an event that indicates that a new business partner has been created within your ERP landscpape:

 

 

Once this subscription has been established you can proactively trigger a workflow or automation task within SAP Build Process Automation. To stay within the example, you can trigger new approval workflows once a new business partner has been established. As a result, every time a new business partner has been entered, an additional approver makes sure that the payment details and to address of the organization has been assigned correctly. By doing so you increase the degree of data accuracy and compliance of your business processes while minimizing the degree of human intervention:

 

Thanks to the native connectivity of SAP Build and our Event Mesh services you can easily select any event to trigger a process instance in SAP Build Process Automation. Further details on how to establish the connectivity can be found on the blogpost of my colleague Win, which can be found here or within the public documentation in the Help-Portal.

 

 

 

3rd scenario: Simplifying connectivity to improve developer efficiency

The final scenario for combining SAP Build and SAP Integration Suite involves our new consolidated API for SAP-managed data: Graph.

Graph uses modern open standards like OData v4 and GraphQL, serving as a key capability within API Management in SAP Integration Suite. With Graph, developers can access your business data as a single, semantically connected data graph that spans the entire suite of SAP products and beyond. It is designed for SAP’s ecosystem of partner and customer developers, offering a powerful API that significantly reduces the cost and complexity of creating and deploying reusable extensions and other client applications.

As enterprise landscapes continue to expand in both scale and complexity, each new system, SaaS, or microservice introduces new protocols, data models, connectivity requirements, and security conventions. Real-world challenges often span multiple lines of business, services, and APIs, making it difficult even for experienced developers to grasp all the underlying technologies and interfaces. Creating new business-extending client applications requires a growing range of expertise and skills. The rise of low-code tools, now widely adopted by nonprofessional developers, further widens this gap.

Graph simplifies this complexity by allowing you to combine and expose data from multiple systems and data sources. You can then easily consume this API within SAP Build. At any point in your SAP Build Process Automation workflow, you can leverage an Action to retrieve, post, patch, put, or delete data within your SAP landscape.

This connectivity model allows you to interact with systems based on a unified enterprise data model, simplifying data access and avoiding unnecessary ETL processes and data replications for specific use cases.

Here you can also find a simplified architecture, that describes this scenario:

 

Additionally, SAP Integration Suite allows you to leverage proven integration features such as API Management, which helps monitor and evaluate your connections. You can also apply critical policies to these connections to ensure compliance and secure operations.

Further details can be found on the blogpost of my colleagues Palak Garg and Evgeniy Gorbunov.

 

 

Conclusion

I hope this blog post has helped you identify the key synergies between SAP Build and SAP Integration Suite and how they can enable seamless automation across your enterprise. Please remember that the use cases provided are just examples and can be tailored and refined to meet your specific needs.

Here you can also find a simplified architecture, that describes the entire scenario in a more generic way:

 For further guidance, I’ve included links to additional blog posts and resources on the Help Portal that will assist you in implementing these use cases. If you're interested in exploring more scenarios related to Enterprise Automation, feel free to check out the following links:

 

  • Official Enterprise Automation with SAP page
  • How to get started with Enterprise Automation Guide
  • Whitepaper: Transition to SAP S/4HANA with Enterprise Automation
  • Enterprise Automation with SAP Product Tour
  • Enterprise Automation with SAP webinar

Stay tuned!

entry.title: How to Approve/Reject Task via API in SAP Build Process Automation
entry.content:
This topic came up recently because of a blog series by @mohit_bansal3 and @Adwait_Fadnavis that explained how to implement approvals via email instead of the Inbox. So the topic of how to approve/reject an approval form via API came up.

Approval Forms

First, it's important to understand how approval forms are created. You can add buttons on approval or notification forms (up to 5 buttons). 

 

And you name them whatever you want, both the label and identifier.

This forms are also know as user tasks, something that requires user intervention in the midst of the process.

 

APIs

Once a task has been triggered, you can then use an API to approve/reject it, or to simulate clicking one of the buttons. You can see when it's waiting to be executed in the Monitoring tab.

You can also see the Instance ID for the task when you expand it, as well as who was assigned it (this is important when we discuss permissions).

To execute (e.g., approve/reject) the task, you go to the Workflow API of SAP Build Process Automation, and then use the /task-instances PATCH call.

The URL path includes the instance ID for the task we saw above. Except for the instance ID, the rest of the URL is shared with everyone in that specific landscape (e.g., eu10). The following is an example URL:

https://spa-api-gateway-bpi-eu-prod.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/workflow/rest 
/v1/task-instances/4005b254-f789-11ef-9cea-eeee0a80bb17  

And in the body, you specify the identifier of the button you want to execute. The status is always "COMPLETED".

{
   "status": "COMPLETED",
   "decision": "a"
}

You can also change other things, like the following (see the spec for the full request body):

  • Due date
  • Recipient of the task
  • Priority

Once you execute the API, the task will be completed, just like as if someone pressed the button.

IMPORTANT: Who is listed as completing the task depends on how you authenticated when calling the API. More on that below.

 

Common Errors

401

Basically, the authorization token is messed up, perhaps because your credentials are wrong or the token got corrupted. 

403

You do not have permission to perform this API (see below).

{
    "error": {
        "code": "bpm.workflowruntime.rest.user.not.sufficient.privileges",
        "message": "User does not have sufficient privileges."
    }
}

404

The Instance ID of the task does not exist.

{
    "error": {
        "code": "bpm.workflowruntime.rest.task.not.found",
        "message": "Task not found",
        "logId": "5f8e6171-4a26-428f-8627-f805b96-eu10"
    }
}

400

You're trying to complete a task that is already completed.

{
    "error": {
        "code": "bpm.workflowruntime.rest.task.final.status",
        "message": "Task is in final status",
        "logId": "cfddd683-a061-4858-8f40-6eee397-eu10",
        "details": [
            {
                "severity": "error",
                "message": "You cannot update the properties of the task with ID 4005b254-e780-11ef-9cea-eeee0a80bb17, since it is in a final status COMPLETED."
            }
        ]
    }
}

422

You've given a wrong status (not COMPLETED).

{
    "error": {
        "code": "bpm.workflowruntime.rest.task.status.invalid",
        "message": "Invalid status requested for task.",
        "logId": "5b05c087-772c-4bee-8a83-ee5e519-eu10"
    }
}

409

You've specified a button that does not exist (I assume "form_approval_1" is named that because it is a form, its identifier is "approval", and it is the first instance of that form in the process).

{
    "error": {
        "message": "No outgoing sequence flow of element 'form_approval_1' could be selected for continuing the process",
        "logId": "ee563964-2dc2-40aa-b9a0-e65208f-eu10"
    }
}

Other errors are shown in the API spec. 

 

Permissions

If you authenticate in a way that requires an authenticated user (e.g., OAuth2UserTokenExchange), then the user must be either an admin or the user who is the current recipient of the form.

  • An admin requires the  ProcessAutomationAdmin role collection.
  • The recipient user must also have the ProcessAutomationParticipant role collection.

In such a case you will the authenticated user as the person who completed the task.

If you authenticate using Client Credentials and just specify the client ID/secret for the API key for the service. you do not need any specific permissions or roles. The form will be shown as being approved by a generic user based on the client ID.

entry.title: Role-based requirement approvals in SAP Cloud ALM with external workflows in SAP BPA
entry.content:
Objectives

This SAP blog post gradually outlines how workflows can be used to extend the approval process of requirements by retrieving team roles from an SAP Cloud ALM project. The workflow management system used in this blog post is the SAP Build Process Automation (abbr. SAP BPA) running on SAP BTP.

Prerequisites

  • You have already provisioned an SAP Cloud ALM tenant and SAP Build Process Automation system, which are both running in your SAP BTP system landscape.
  • The connection between SAP Cloud ALM and SAP Build Process Automation was already established via Destinations, Webhooks and Subscriptions to transmit Task API data bidirectionally. If this is not yet done, please review the previous SAP blog post to implement all necessary steps.
  • Additionally, an SMTP mail server destination is configured in your SAP BTP subaccount to receive emails from SAP BPA.
  • A Desktop Agent to execute the automated workflow on SAP BPA is already installed on your local computer.
  • You have been assigned at least the role collections ProcessAutomationAdmin and ProcessAutomationDeveloper in your SAP BTP subaccount.

Procedure Overview

  • Define a custom project team role in SAP Cloud ALM.
  • How the workflow solution design looks like in SAP BPA.
  • Overview about which REST APIs are needed and how they work.
  • How to fetch members of a project role in the subsequent workflow.

Define project team role in SAP Cloud ALM

In the first step, you must go to your SAP Cloud ALM project and add another custom project role which should be called “Requirement Approver”. Following that, this new custom role must be assigned to your project in the Teams tab. Afterwards, assign the corresponding users who should review the requirement later by accepting or rejecting the approval document in SAP BPA. I have assigned two demo users here, however there could be multiple and different approver roles per project.

Workflow Solution design

Let’s focus on the design of the workflow solution in SAP BPA. At the beginning of the process, a trigger is initiated by an event from SAP Cloud ALM that requests the requirement’s data. The outputs of the API trigger are automatically synchronized with the process attributes in SAP BPA. The task details of the requirement are retrieved from SAP Cloud ALM via an HTTP GET method. The task is being identified by its ID via the API connection.

On the level of the second step, the attribute “projectID” which was transmitted via the API from SAP Cloud ALM, must be specified as input variable in the step details. The other two variables “RoleName” and “TeamName” must be manually entered since these aren’t transferred via the API. Those variables will be checked in the subsequent workflow (see next chapter), whether they match.

 

Following this step, the workflow then checks in a branch condition whether the submitted list from the subsequent workflow is empty, in which the users of the project role “Requirement Approver” from SAP Cloud ALM would normally have been listed. Additionally, the branch condition assesses if the requirement status has been set to “In Approval”. Here, the technical name of the requirement status, coming from the API, must be stated.

If one of the two branch conditions hasn’t been met, e.g. if the requirement has the status “Blocked”, the workflow will abort, and an error email will be forwarded to the user who triggered the approval workflow. The approval or rejection of the requirement will be issued via an HTTP POST method to set the status of the task accordingly.

Continuing in the workflow structure, the corresponding approver(s) will receive an email to proceed to the SAP Build Process Automation Inbox. From there they must complete the Requirement Approval document and either approve or reject the requirement manually.

Overview of Rest APIs and their usage

Reading multiple members of a project role from SAP Cloud ALM works by using several REST API interfaces provided from the SAP Business Accelerator Hub. The relevant API are SAP Cloud ALM Tasks and SAP Cloud ALM Projects.

First, the Task API provides the necessary data record which includes the Project ID and is essential to fetch all teams of an SAP Cloud ALM project. This team in SAP Cloud ALM contains multiple project roles, however only the own created “Requirement Approver” project role is relevant. After given the reference from the Task API to the Project ID, all roles of the given PMO team must be finally read out. The assigned users of the project role “Requirement Approver” are listed with their email address in the transmitted Team Roles API.

How to fetch members of a project role

After given this high-level overview about how project roles can be read out with the usage of REST APIs, I would like to focus on some deeper insights into the step “Get Members of Role in Team” from the Workflow Solution Design.

The subsequent process workflow starts by defining an action, which calls all teams of the transmitted project id via API. An empty list will be created to save all the found project members later. After that, the workflow loops through each team of the given project from SAP Cloud ALM and checks if the current team’s name is equal to the team’s name (= “PMO Team”) specified in the parameter section from the Workflow Solution Diagram.

In the affirmative case which highlights that the PMO team was found in the SAP Cloud ALM project, another action is triggered to get all the project roles of this PMO team.

Another for-loop decision traverses through all the roles of the PMO team and checks if the input (= “Requirement Approver”), is found and meets the expected value. Based on this decision, the empty list will be filled with the user’s email address. As soon as the desired project role is found, the for-loop should end the workflow.

Let’s also talk about the termination mechanism. For instance, if the “Requirement Approver” project role wasn’t found in the SAP Cloud ALM team, because it wasn’t either created or no users were maintained in a project role, then several abortions were inserted to prevent an endless loop.

Result

After triggering the request approval status of the requirement in SAP Cloud ALM, the users of the project role “Requirement Approver” will receive an email asking them to review the requirement.

They will get forwarded to the SAP BPA Inbox, where the respective stakeholder can review the and either approve or reject the requirement.

The result of the requirement review process will be updated in SAP Cloud ALM.

Conclusion

For organizations requiring a more customized workflow solution for their requirements in SAP Cloud ALM together with SAP Build Process Automation, my SAP Consulting department offers a consulting service, which can assist in implementing advanced configurations for workflows. Please contact us via the following email to ask about the workflow integration solution: alm.consulting@sap.com.

With these upcoming developments, SAP Cloud ALM is set to become an even more powerful tool for managing workflows and approvals, offering enhanced flexibility and automation capabilities.
